title.en: Tool Use, Agents & MCP: Claude in Your Systems

description.en: Understand Claude's tool-use loop, the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as the open standard for connecting AI to systems, and where Claude Code and computer use fit in the agent stack.

story_variants.en:
## From Answers to Actions

A model that only writes text is a consultant; a model that can *call your systems* is a coworker. **Tool use** (function calling) is the mechanism that makes the difference, and it works the same conceptual way across Claude, GPT and Gemini — but Anthropic pushed the pattern furthest by standardizing the *plumbing* with the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** and shipping flagship agent products (Claude Code, computer use) on top of it.

## The Tool-Use Loop

Here is the crucial mental model: **the model never executes anything.** It only *requests* calls; your code runs them. The loop:

1. You send a request that includes **tool definitions** — name, description, and a JSON Schema for parameters.
2. Claude decides a tool is needed and returns a `tool_use` block: the tool's name plus arguments it filled in.
3. **Your code** looks the tool up, validates the arguments, executes the real function, and sends the result back as a `tool_result` block in a new user turn.
4. Claude reads the result and either answers or requests another call. Repeat until done.

That dispatch step — *name in, function out, execute, return result* — is the beating heart of every agent framework, and it is what you will implement in the challenge. Two production rules follow directly:

- **Validate everything.** Tool arguments are model output: well-schooled but not guaranteed. Treat them like untrusted user input.
- **Handle unknown tools gracefully.** Models occasionally hallucinate tool names or call retired ones; a dispatcher must return a clean error, not crash the loop.

> **Callout:** Tool descriptions are prompts. A vague description ("gets data") produces vague tool selection. Write descriptions as if onboarding a sharp new hire: what it does, when to use it, what the parameters mean.

## MCP: The USB-C Moment

Before late 2024, every team hand-wired every tool to every model — N models × M systems = N×M integrations. Anthropic's answer was the **Model Context Protocol**, an open standard (donated to open governance) that turns the mess into N + M:

- An **MCP server** wraps a system — GitHub, Postgres, Slack, your internal CRM — and exposes *tools*, *resources* and *prompts* in a standard schema.
- An **MCP client** (Claude Desktop, Claude Code, IDEs, or your own app) connects to any server and discovers its capabilities at runtime.

The strategic surprise: **competitors adopted it.** OpenAI and Google announced MCP support in their own stacks, which is the strongest possible signal that an interoperability standard has won. For enterprises this matters enormously — an MCP server you build for your ticketing system is an investment that outlives any single vendor choice.

| Layer | What it is | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Model | Reasons, requests tool calls | Claude Sonnet/Opus 4.x |
| Tool-use API | Structured call/result protocol | tool_use / tool_result blocks |
| MCP | Standard for packaging tools | GitHub MCP server, internal CRM server |
| Agent product | Loop + policy + UX | Claude Code, computer use |

Be balanced: MCP adds real operational surface. Every server is code you must secure, version and monitor, and a malicious or compromised MCP server is a serious threat vector (tool descriptions can smuggle prompt injections). Enterprises should maintain an *allowlist* of vetted servers, not let every team connect anything.

## Claude Code and Computer Use

Two Anthropic products show what the stack enables:

- **Claude Code** is an agentic coding tool: it reads repositories, edits files, runs tests and iterates — a tool-use loop specialized for software engineering, with MCP as its extension mechanism. Enterprises use it for migrations, test coverage, and codebase Q&A; the discipline it demands is *review culture* — generated diffs get reviewed like any other engineer's.
- **Computer use** lets Claude drive a screen: it looks at screenshots and emits clicks and keystrokes. It is the most general integration (anything a human can operate) and the least efficient one — slower and less reliable than an API call. The professional heuristic: **API first, MCP second, computer use last**, for legacy systems with no other door.

> **Adoption pitfall:** Teams hear "agents" and reach for maximum autonomy immediately. Start the other way: human-approves-every-action, then human-approves-risky-actions, then autonomous-with-audit-log. Ratchet autonomy up only as evals and incident history justify it.

## Designing a Tool Belt

Good agents come from good tool design more than clever prompts. Keep tools **few and orthogonal** (a dozen crisp tools beat fifty overlapping ones), make destructive operations **separate tools** with explicit names (`delete_record`, never a `mode` flag on `update_record`), return **structured, compact results** (models read them as tokens — a 200KB JSON dump is a cost and confusion bomb), and log every call/result pair for audit.

In the challenge you will build the dispatcher: a registry of Python functions, a loop over model-issued calls expressed as dicts, execution with keyword arguments, and graceful handling of an unknown tool. Fifteen lines that mirror exactly what Claude's agent loop — and every serious framework — does under the hood.

description.en: The model requests; your dispatcher executes; MCP standardizes where tools come from; Claude Code and computer use are products built on the same loop.

left.label: Hand-wire every tool to every model (N×M integrations)

right.label: Wrap systems as MCP servers once; any MCP client connects (N+M)

description.en: MCP's economics: integrations become reusable infrastructure instead of per-project glue — now supported across vendor ecosystems.

explanation.en: The registry maps tool names to real Python functions — the only place execution is authorized.
Look up by the model-provided name; .get() returns None instead of crashing on hallucinated tools.
Unknown tools produce a clean error result the model can read and recover from.
**call['args'] unpacks the model's arguments as keyword parameters — after validation in real systems.

description.en: A dispatch run: two successful tool calls and one hallucinated tool handled gracefully instead of crashing the agent loop.
The autonomy ratchet: earn each level with eval results and incident history, never start at the top.

instruction.en: Build the tool-call dispatcher at the heart of every agent loop. Complete dispatch(calls, registry): for each call dict {'name': ..., 'args': {...}} in order, look the name up in registry; if missing, append the string 'error: unknown tool <name>' to the results; otherwise execute the function with **args and append str(result). Return the results list. Then run dispatch(CALLS, REGISTRY) and print EXACTLY four lines: each result on its own line in order, then 'handled=' plus the number of calls processed. Models sometimes hallucinate tool names — a dispatcher that survives that is the difference between a recovered turn and a crashed agent.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
def get_weather(city):
    return f"{city}:sunny"

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

REGISTRY = {"get_weather": get_weather, "add": add}

CALLS = [
    {"name": "get_weather", "args": {"city": "Chennai"}},
    {"name": "add", "args": {"a": 2, "b": 3}},
    {"name": "delete_db", "args": {}},
]

def dispatch(calls, registry):
    results = []
    # TODO: for each call, look up call["name"] in registry
    # TODO: unknown tool -> append "error: unknown tool <name>"
    # TODO: known tool -> execute fn(**call["args"]) and append str(result)
    return results

# TODO: run dispatch(CALLS, REGISTRY), print each result line,
# then print "handled=<number of calls>"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
Chennai:sunny
5
error: unknown tool delete_db
handled=3

en: Use registry.get(call["name"]) — it returns None for unknown tools instead of raising KeyError.
Execute with fn(**call["args"]) so the dict of arguments becomes keyword parameters, and wrap the result in str().
After the loop: for r in results: print(r), then print(f"handled={len(CALLS)}").

practice_challenges.test_code:
out = dispatch(CALLS, REGISTRY)
assert out == ["Chennai:sunny", "5", "error: unknown tool delete_db"]
assert dispatch([], REGISTRY) == []
assert dispatch([{"name": "add", "args": {"a": 10, "b": 32}}], REGISTRY) == ["42"]
assert dispatch([{"name": "nope", "args": {}}], REGISTRY) == ["error: unknown tool nope"]

q.en: In Claude's tool-use loop, who actually executes the tool?

en: Claude executes it inside Anthropic's infrastructure
Your application code — the model only requests calls with arguments
The MCP registry executes it automatically

q.en: What core problem does the Model Context Protocol solve?

en: It compresses prompts to reduce token costs
It replaces the need for tool use entirely
It standardizes tool/resource integrations so N models and M systems need N+M connectors instead of N×M

q.en: What is the recommended integration priority for connecting an agent to a legacy system?

en: Computer use first, since it is most general
API first, MCP second, computer use as the last resort

q.en: Why must a production dispatcher handle tool names that are not in its registry?

en: Registries are rebuilt on every request
Models can hallucinate or call retired tool names; the loop must return a clean error instead of crashing
The API randomly renames tools for load balancing
Unknown tools indicate the API key expired
